agent_raw: The fraud ranking model comparison presented to management is not valid evidence for the migration decision. The claimed six-basis-point PR-AUC gain (0.214 to 0.220) is confounded by two factors that each independently invalidate the comparison. First, the baseline was evaluated using 30-day provisional fraud labels while the challenger used 90-day matured labels. Fraud chargebacks and disputes resolve over time; 90-day labels are a more complete representation of actual fraud than 30-day labels. Evaluating the two models against different label standards means the PR-AUC values are not measuring the same thing. Some or all of the 0.006 difference may reflect richer labels for the challenger rather than better model performance. Second, the evaluations used different time periods — Q1 for the baseline and Q2 for the challenger — and a merchant onboarding campaign changed the transaction mix and geography between those quarters. Population shift between evaluation windows further confounds the comparison. The temporal split approach (by week, forward-looking) is sound and is not the issue. The fix is to evaluate both models on the same quarter with matched 90-day labels and to report a paired bootstrap confidence interval for the PR-AUC difference. Until that corrected comparison is available, the migration decision has no valid evidentiary basis.
all_issues_raised: Label maturation mismatch: 30-day baseline vs. 90-day challenger PR-AUC
Different evaluation quarters with merchant onboarding population shift
No confidence interval for small PR-AUC gain at 0.2% fraud prevalence
